cited_competitors: belzutifan; Peloton Labs; Dr. Brigirulis; Chen et al.; Reger's lab; UT Southwestern

talk_title: Molecular glue degrader targeting the CCNE1
crispro_top_description: While CCNE1 amplification is a strong biomarker, some amplified cell lines show reduced sensitivity, potentially due to P16 expression or other factors.
cited_competitors: palbociclib; CDK2 inhibitors; Wee1 inhibitor; PKMYT1 inhibitor

talk_title: Molecular glue degrader of the RNA binding protein HuR
crispro_top_description: HuR dependency is strongly correlated with BRAF mutations, suggesting BRAF mutant CRC as a key indication, but other BRAF mutant cancers may also be sensitive.
cited_competitors: dabrafenib; anti-EGFR; BRAF inhibitor; MEK inhibitor

talk_title: RAS PI3K alpha breaker
crispro_top_description: BB010203 shows strong combination potential with HER2-targeted agents (tucatinib, trastuzumab) in HER2-amplified models, potentially overcoming shortcomings of existing therapies.

talk_title: Catalytic inhibitor of the KAT2A/B protein
crispro_top_description: KAT2 inhibition upregulates interferon responses, suggesting potential for combination with immune-oncology therapies like BCMA CD3 bispecific antibodies.
cited_competitors: HDAC inhibitors; bromodomain inhibitors; lenalidomide; pomalidomide; dexamethasone; BCMA CD3 bispecific antibody

talk_title: Discovery of a new class of mutant-targeted catalytic RAS oncogene inhibitor
crispro_top_description: RMO-55 demonstrates activity in models of acquired resistance to RAS on inhibitors, particularly those involving KRAS amplification or increased expression.
cited_competitors: sotrastaurin; adagrasib; deraxone rasip; RMO-55; cyclophilin; CRAF; RMC-6236; HaCaTs

talk_title: CQ0736 polysaccharide-based KRAS pan‑targeted therapy
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "No acquired resistance observed during treatment." | Speaker said: "We will not trigger anti‑drug problem because we hide from the immune system."
crispro_top_description: Macropinocytosis uptake varies 5‑10‑fold between KRAS‑mutant and wild‑type cells; CrisPRO could develop a predictive biomarker panel for high macropinocytosis tumors.

talk_title: RNA‑targeted small molecules inhibiting c‑MYC translation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We do not affect levels of the Myc mRNA transcript in these cells, which is also consistent with a translational mechanism." | Speaker said: "We see a little bit of lymphopenia, but this has been a major challenge in the field of Myc‑targeting therapies."
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed rapid MYC protein degradation within 30 min, then concluded that the compound is broadly tolerable with no body‑weight loss, despite early immune toxicity signals.
mechanistic_blindspot: Lymphopenia observed in mice suggests off‑target impact on immune cells not captured by in‑vitro selectivity assays.
crispro_top_description: Compound activity is nutrient‑dependent; CrisPRO could develop a biomarker of tumor nutrient deprivation to select responsive patients.

talk_title: Cbl‑B intramolecular glue inhibitors (NX‑1607) for T‑cell activation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We see robust efficacy at significantly lower doses" | Speaker said: "We have a very potent Kd of 0.4 nM, driven by a slow off rate"
crispro_top_description: Identify patients with high phospho‑HS1 or low Cbl‑B activity to enrich for responders.

talk_title: NEO811 molecular glue degrader of ARNT
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "NEO811 shows superior potency to Belzutifan" | Speaker said: "Compound C mitigates off‑target degradation of SALL4"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that ARNT undergoes a large conformational rearrangement upon glue binding, yet concluded that the same glue chemistry can be applied broadly without further structural optimization.
crispro_top_description: Develop a transcriptional HIF‑target signature to select patients likely to benefit from ARNT degradation.

talk_title: DNA‑templated spatially controlled PROTACs (DTAG) for CDK4/6‑Cyclin D1 degradation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Version one able to induce CDK4, CDK6, as well as the Cyclin D1 degradation" | Speaker said: "The mouse body doesn't alter a lot, which indicates no severe side effect observed"
cognitive_dissonance: Molecular dynamics suggested stable ternary complexes for all three versions, yet version three (largest distance) showed no degradation in cells.
crispro_top_description: CrisPRO can model optimal ligand spacing on DNA to predict degradation efficiency, offering a computational design advantage over empirical DTAG screening.

talk_title: BH‑501284 non‑covalent pan‑KRAS inhibitor
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "1284 demonstrated picomolar binding affinity with more than 54‑hour resident time" | Speaker said: "1284 achieved deep tumor regression even at a 5 mg/kg BID dose level"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that 1284 binds both GDP‑ and GTP‑bound KRAS with picomolar affinity yet claimed selectivity over HRAS/NRAS despite structural similarity of the switch‑II pocket.
mechanistic_blindspot: Lymphopenia observed in mice suggests immune‑cell impact not captured in tumor models.
crispro_top_description: EGFR ligand rescue reduces efficacy of many KRAS inhibitors; CrisPRO can model EGFR‑driven feedback to design combination strategies.

talk_title: Proteomics‑guided discovery of TRIM21‑mediated multimeric degradation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "ACE can induce TRIM21‑dependent degradation, is selectivity, and only enriches with NPC proteins in proteasome‑dependent manner." | Speaker said: "Only the multimeric proteins can activate the TRIM21 and direct its proteasome degradation."
cognitive_dissonance: Global proteomics showed selective NPC degradation, yet the speaker suggested broad applicability to other multimeric targets without presenting data on off‑target immune activation.
crispro_top_description: Identify tumors with high expression of multimeric complexes (e.g., NPC components) to predict ACE responsiveness.

talk_title: MUTA platform for multifunctional ADCs
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we saw 150 ADCs didn't make it in the clinic, predominantly solid tumor targets, and they fall in this bucket of 50,000 or low copies" | Speaker said: "we improved the safety as well, no findings at one mg or three mg per kg, but reversible reticulocyte decrease at nine mg per kg"
failing_target: Conventional monospecific ADCs targeting low‑copy solid‑tumor antigens
mechanistic_blindspot: Heterogeneous low antigen copy number limits internalization and therapeutic window
crispro_top_description: Need for quantitative biomarkers to identify patients with sufficient co‑expression of the two antigens (e.g., MUC16 and NaPi2b) to benefit from MUDA ADCs

talk_title: High‑throughput drug screening & single‑cell network analysis in IDH mutant glioma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we identified three distinct vulnerability programs, each actionable with existing compounds" | Speaker said: "current standard of care achieves disease stabilization but is not curative"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that IDH inhibition stabilizes disease, yet concluded that "current therapy is not curative" without presenting data on long‑term survival
mechanistic_blindspot: Cell‑state heterogeneity driven by master regulator programs (e.g., Hes4‑Notch, EGFR‑Neuroligin‑1) that are not targeted by IDH inhibition alone
crispro_top_description: Target master regulator‑driven cell states (e.g., DNA‑damage repair in AC‑like, MAPK in NPC‑like) with existing drugs identified by OncoTreat

institution: Chinese research team (collaboration with McMaster, etc.)
talk_title: Serum hyaluronic acid as predictive biomarker of T‑DXD‑induced interstitial lung disease
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "T‑DXD induced ILD rate is about 15% across all tumor types, fatal event rate about 2.2%" | Speaker said: "In young mice ILD rate is 0% compared with 40% in adult mice"
failing_target: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T‑DXD) – ADC‑induced interstitial lung disease
mechanistic_blindspot: Age‑dependent accumulation in alveolar macrophages leading to HYAL2‑mediated hyaluronic acid release
crispro_top_description: Implement serum HA (alone or in panel) as a prospective companion diagnostic to exclude high‑risk patients before T‑DXD initiation

talk_title: Co‑clinical trial of datopotamab deruxtecan (Dato) in patient‑derived xenografts
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Overall, Dato significantly extended time to tumor doubling in 13 of the 14 pretreatment models" | Speaker said: "Combination of Dato and gemcitabine was highly effective at controlling tumor growth"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented robust PDX‑patient concordance (78.6%) yet admitted that 22% of pairs were discordant, highlighting a gap between model fidelity and clinical prediction
failing_target: Dato monotherapy in Trop‑2 low‑expressing tumors
mechanistic_blindspot: Reliance on Trop‑2 expression; two progressive‑disease models were Trop‑2 negative yet showed limited response
crispro_top_description: Develop a quantitative Trop‑2 expression cutoff using CrisPRO to select patients most likely to benefit from Dato

talk_title: Inducer‑amplifier strategy for TP53‑mutant cancers
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The interaction was synergistic specifically in TP53 mutant and knockout cells" | Speaker said: "Adding a WEE1 inhibitor after the priming increased cell death from 30% to nearly 80%"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that PARP inhibition alone increased DNA damage but did not cause cell death, yet concluded that adding WEE1 inhibitor converts damage to lethal mitotic catastrophe
failing_target: PARP inhibitor monotherapy in TP53‑mutant tumors
mechanistic_blindspot: TP53 loss leads to reliance on base excision repair; PARP inhibition alone does not induce lethal DNA damage without additional replication stress
crispro_top_description: Reposition existing PARP inhibitors for TP53‑mutant cancers using the inducer‑amplifier framework

talk_title: KBD‑1111: a selective, long‑acting, brain‑penetrant PARP‑1 inhibitor
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "KBD‑1111 shows 9,000‑fold selectivity for PARP‑1 over PARP‑2" | Speaker said: "In rats, QW dosing showed no significant reticulocyte decrease, unlike QD dosing"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented superior in‑vitro trapping data yet could not provide PD data to explain the claimed Q2W dosing advantage
mechanistic_blindspot: PARP‑2 inhibition drives hematologic toxicity (anemia, thrombocytopenia)
crispro_top_description: Leverage KBD‑1111’s brain penetration to target PARP‑dependent DNA repair in CNS metastases where first‑gen PARP inhibitors fail

talk_title: Comparative activity of TEAD central‑pocket inhibitor vs. YAP‑TEAD PPI in KRAS‑mutant cancers
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The combination of the Yap/TED PPI with sotorasib gave a much more durable and pronounced extended response" | Speaker said: "Both inhibitors were able to provide similar therapeutic benefit in vivo"
cognitive_dissonance: In vitro data showed superior durability of the PPI versus the CPI, yet in vivo xenograft models both modalities yielded comparable tumor regression
failing_target: KRAS G12C monotherapy (sotorasib) in resistant cell lines
mechanistic_blindspot: Hippo pathway activation (YAP/TAZ‑TEAD transcription) bypasses KRAS blockade
crispro_top_description: Pair KRAS G12C inhibitors with TEAD PPI (IAG 933) in KRAS‑mutant NSCLC to overcome Hippo‑mediated resistance
cited_competitors: AZD0751 (central‑pocket TEAD inhibitor); Sotorasib (KRAS G12C inhibitor)

institution: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center
talk_title: RAS and RAF targeting in MSS metastatic colorectal cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we don't have that data formally yet" | Speaker said: "I don't think that MSS will be solved by a single drug, but it will be solved with strategy" | Speaker said: "we still don't have great ideas yet or at least great signals of what to do afterwards"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed pre‑clinical data that EGFR inhibition alone is inactive in BRAF‑mutant CRC, yet concluded that adding EGFR to BRAF inhibition is sufficient for durable pathway inhibition without needing additional targets
mechanistic_blindspot: Emergence of KRAS, NRAS, MAP2K1 mutations and MET amplifications at progression
crispro_top_description: Baseline high cell‑cycle transcriptional signature predicts emergence of secondary KRAS/NRAS mutations; CrisPRO could develop a predictive model to select patients who need upfront combination with PI3K or MET inhibitors
cited_competitors: Vemurafenib; Encorafenib; Sotorasib; Adagrasib; Pluxerafenib; Amivantamab
companies_to_monitor: MD Anderson (academic program); BridgeBio; Amgen; Astellas

institution: Monell Chemical Senses Center / University of Pennsylvania
talk_title: Chemosensory dysfunction in cancer: prevalence, impact and unmet needs
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Currently, we don't have pharmacological solutions to reverse the smell dysfunction or taste dysfunction that really works." | Speaker said: "We don't have good data in the EMR either, because in time‑pressured environments it is often deprioritized." | Speaker said: "There is no wonder drug here... the best we can do right now is nutrition counseling and self‑management strategies."
mechanistic_blindspot: lack of mechanistic understanding of how cancer therapies cause persistent taste and smell loss
crispro_top_description: Absence of validated biomarkers to identify patients at highest risk for chemosensory dysfunction, limiting targeted preventive or therapeutic interventions.

talk_title: Taste bud homeostasis, cancer therapeutics and organoid models
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We don't fully understand why a steady rundown of taste buds causes dysgeusia so quickly in patients." | Speaker said: "We still lack in vitro models for human taste epithelium." | Speaker said: "The smoothened antagonist knocks out taste buds, yet it is highly effective against cancer – a paradoxical effect."
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that smoothened antagonists efficiently shrink tumors, then concluded that these drugs cause severe loss of taste buds – a contradiction between tumor efficacy and taste toxicity.
mechanistic_blindspot: off‑target impact on taste progenitor proliferation leading to excess type‑I cells and loss of sweet perception
crispro_top_description: Identify and model off‑target KIT inhibition in taste progenitors to design selective TKIs that spare taste function.

talk_title: Technical considerations and architecture of the Genie Data Model
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the Genie data model is open source, cohort agnostic, and the really key point here is it implements a dual pathway architecture" | Speaker said: "we looked to FHIR and mCODE for interoperability components, and then also the analytical framework of the OMOP Common Data Model"
crispro_top_description: Develop AI/NLP pipelines to accurately extract clinical variables from unstructured notes, addressing the manual abstraction gap highlighted by the speaker.
cited_competitors: FHIR; mCODE; OMOP Common Data Model

talk_title: Implementation of the Genie Data Model at Providence (GP3)
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "GP3 aims to replace or augment the manual data curation process so we can scale to larger cohorts and have better data quality" | Speaker said: "We use AI tools like GitHub Copilot to accelerate code writing and improve software engineering best practices"
crispro_top_description: Apply CrisPRO's data lineage and validation framework to enhance GP3's QA/QC pipelines for heterogeneous source hierarchies.

talk_title: European perspective on Genie, national cancer nodes, and clinical implementation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Genie has shown that multi-institutional data sharing is feasible, and with these layers of data sharing and the Candle project, the EU is now trying to scale this up to up to 20 nations" | Speaker said: "In the GenomeDE project, health insurance pays for sequencing only after a confirmation token is returned, creating a financial incentive for data submission"
crispro_top_description: Leverage CrisPRO to provide standardized QA/QC metrics for the German cBioPortal MTB extension, addressing the speaker's concern about internal validation.
companies_to_monitor: University of Lübeck; German Cancer Consortium

talk_title: Functional significance of M6A readers YTHDF1/2/3 in acute myeloid leukemia
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: we haven't looked into that. | Speaker said: I don't have a clue.
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that YTHDF2 deletion expands normal hematopoietic stem cells (no phenotype) yet concluded that YTHDF2 inhibition will be a safe therapeutic strategy without presenting long‑term toxicity data
mechanistic_blindspot: low potency and lack of selectivity for YTHDF2 versus other YTHDF paralogs
crispro_top_description: TP53‑mutant AML appears especially sensitive to YTHDF3 knockdown, but the effect in TP53‑wildtype AML is unclear

institution: University of Chicago, Department of Chemistry
talk_title: M6A readers in tumor microenvironment and chromatin regulation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We haven't done that much R‑loop correlation with M6A" | Speaker said: "I won't be surprised, there's a correlation"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented extensive preclinical data showing YTHDC1 inhibition collapses stemness in neuroendocrine cancers, yet concluded that the same inhibitor "gave us the best result" without providing comparative pharmacodynamic or safety data
mechanistic_blindspot: Limited in‑vivo potency and lack of pharmacokinetic data for solid‑tumor models
crispro_top_description: Unclear whether YTHDC1 inhibition affects DNA damage response pathways (e.g., ATM, PARP) in solid tumors

talk_title: Precision diets targeting serine and glycine metabolism
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we do see this effect in many cancer cells, but not all" | Speaker said: "there is absolutely no impact of the serine/glycine-free diet" for the RAS p53 model | Speaker said: "we noticed that that did result in weight loss in the mice that had to be managed quite carefully" | Speaker said: "long‑term serine and glycine depletion in mice ... can lead to peripheral neuropathy" | Speaker said: "we were not able to see any changes in T cell infiltration"
mechanistic_blindspot: Upregulation of de novo serine synthesis via PHGDH renders tumors independent of exogenous serine/glycine
crispro_top_description: Identify patients with low PHGDH expression or loss of serine synthesis enzymes to enrich for responders to serine/glycine restriction.

talk_title: Exercise‑derived extracellular vesicles suppress metastasis
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the number of particles doubles during exercise" | Speaker said: "the EV fraction from the exercise mouse had a profound effect on cell proliferation and cell viability" | Speaker said: "the answer was yes" when asked if EVs recapitulate exercise benefit | Speaker said: "we didn't see anything remarkable in the immune system"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that EMT proteins are suppressed by exercise yet concluded that "most of the effects with exercise are through the immune system" – contradictory because immune data were negative
crispro_top_description: Develop a CrisPRO‑guided computational pipeline to identify additional exercise‑induced EV proteins that suppress metastasis beyond MTSS1/NME2.

talk_title: Harnessing diet‑microbiome interactions for colorectal cancer prevention
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "microbiome changes are subtle in early polyps and become strong in CRC" | Speaker said: "coffee stands out as the factor most strongly associated with the microbiome" | Speaker said: "heavy coffee drinkers had a much better survival among colon cancer patients" | Speaker said: "we are conducting a randomized control trial with encapsulated instant coffee"
mechanistic_blindspot: Causality cannot be inferred from retrospective case‑control microbiome studies
crispro_top_description: Use CrisPRO to develop a microbiome‑based risk score that identifies patients who would benefit most from coffee intervention.
cited_competitors: World Cancer Research Fund / AICR; Nurses' Health Study II
companies_to_monitor: Companies developing microbiome‑based diagnostics; Coffee supplement manufacturers entering clinical trials

talk_title: Mutational Epidemiology and the Role of Promoters in Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the vast majority of chemicals that the National Toxicology Program and IARC have classified as being potential carcinogens, they are not mutagenic as far as we can tell" | Speaker noted: "we found a truly resounding negative" when no new mutational signature explained geographic variation in esophageal cancer
mechanistic_blindspot: Promotion mechanisms are not captured by mutational signature analysis
crispro_top_description: Develop computational biomarkers to detect promoter-driven clonal expansion in normal tissues

talk_title: Promotion, Selectogens and Clonal Competition in Cancer Initiation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the promotion step, in other words, the inflammation, was actually the rate‑limiting step" | Speaker noted: "only three of the carcinogens showed any mutational signatures"
mechanistic_blindspot: Differential clonal competition not accounted for in current models
crispro_top_description: Use CrisPRO to model clonal competition dynamics and predict which promoter‑driven clones will expand

institution: Brigham and Women's Hospital / Harvard Medical School
talk_title: Epigenetic Reprogramming and Combination Therapy in Triple‑Negative Breast Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "EZH2 and AKT inhibitors together increase the expression of normal mature luminal markers" | Speaker admitted: "we don't think EZH2 or PI3K pathway alterations are useful biomarkers"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed scRNA‑seq data indicating strong basal/mesenchymal signatures in resistant cells, then concluded that EZH2/AKT inhibition can be broadly applied to TNBC without biomarker selection
mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of single‑agent activity in TNBC despite high EZH2 expression
crispro_top_description: Use CrisPRO to develop a predictive expression signature (mesenchymal vs basal) for EZH2/AKT combination response

talk_title: Mechanisms and Subtypes of Cancer Cachexia
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "high‑fat diet actually worsens cancer cachexia" | Speaker noted: "serotonin reuptake inhibitor improves depression but does not improve weight loss"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that correcting serotonin deficiency rescues depression in mice, yet concluded that this does not improve overall cachexia outcomes
mechanistic_blindspot: Prostaglandin E2 production in tumor driven by high‑fat diet exacerbates wasting via vagal signaling
crispro_top_description: Develop CrisPRO‑driven multi‑omics signatures to separate inflammatory (high GDF15/IL‑6) from non‑inflammatory cachexia patients

talk_title: Neutrophil extracellular traps drive tumor necrosis and metastasis
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "when you target the NETs, we reduce tumor necrosis, but we also reduce metastasis" | Speaker said: "we don't know whether the neutrophils are driving the heart disease, we just observed the phenotype"
crispro_top_description: Targeting NET formation (e.g., with disulfiram or DNase 1) could synergize with immunotherapy by reducing necrosis‑driven metastasis.

talk_title: Ischemic arginase‑1 macrophages suppress T‑cell killing via acetylated polyamines
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we found a hundredfold increase in N1‑acetylspermidine in ischemic macrophages" | Speaker said: "the metabolite severely affect T cell killing of tumors"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that macrophages in ischemic zones upregulate arginase‑1 (a metabolic enzyme) yet concluded that the secreted acetylated polyamine is the primary immunosuppressive factor, ignoring the broader metabolic reprogramming of arginine metabolism.
mechanistic_blindspot: PAOX‑mediated conversion of N1‑acetylspermidine to a T‑cell inhibitory metabolite
crispro_top_description: Inhibit PAOX or block N1‑acetylspermidine production to restore T‑cell cytotoxicity in ischemic tumor regions.

talk_title: Tumor‑derived angiopoietin‑like 7 drives necrotic niche formation and metastatic dissemination
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "knockdown of angiopoietin‑like 7 drastically reduces necrosis and CTC dissemination" | Speaker said: "necrotic cores are not passive; tumor cells actively produce proteins that promote necrosis"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated that necrotic zones contain viable perinecrotic tumor cells producing ANGPTL7, yet concluded that necrosis is an active driver of metastasis, overlooking that the core itself is largely dead.
mechanistic_blindspot: Regulation of TGF‑β abundance and neutrophil/macrophage polarization within necrotic zones
crispro_top_description: Develop ANGPTL7 neutralizing antibodies or small molecules to block necrosis‑driven metastasis.

talk_title: Translational rheostat drives cancer metastasis
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: we thought that if you get rid of something that's a tumor suppressor, metastasis should go up, not down | Speaker said: we don't fully understand why some patients escaped
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that 4E‑BP1 acts as a tumor suppressor for primary tumor growth, then concluded that 4E‑BP1 stimulates metastasis – contradictory roles for the same protein
mechanistic_blindspot: Paradoxical up‑regulation of weak‑mRNA translation (e.g., pannexin‑1) by a translational suppressor whose mechanism is not explained
crispro_top_description: 4E‑BP1 status (high vs low) could stratify patients at risk of metastasis despite primary‑tumor suppression

talk_title: Dicodon organization links tRNA modification to cancer proteome
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: we only explained about 15% of the regulation with single codon usage, suggesting missing mechanisms | Speaker said: the more dicodons that an mRNA had, the less efficient translation initiation it has
mechanistic_blindspot: Dicodon context and spacing effects on elongation are not fully captured by current models
crispro_top_description: High ELP3 expression or presence of specific dicodon signatures could identify tumors dependent on tRNA modification

talk_title: Selective translation drives drug tolerance in breast cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: mRNA level can predict only around 30% of the cancer proteome | Speaker said: we see a beautiful correlation between the KMK2D protein and the eIF4A protein
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that ERα is a transcription factor, then concluded that its RNA‑binding activity (a non‑transcriptional function) is essential for drug tolerance – contradictory emphasis on transcription vs. translation
mechanistic_blindspot: Calcium channel blockade alone does not eradicate therapy‑surviving cells; dependence on KMK2D‑driven translation remains
crispro_top_description: KMK2D protein levels could be used to select patients likely to benefit from zotatifin or verapamil combinations
companies_to_monitor: Company developing zotatifin (e.g., LEO Pharma); Pharma pursuing ELP3 or tRNA‑modifying enzyme programs

session_title: Therapeutic Advances in Antibody Drug Conjugates
talk_title: CTEP Trial Number 10355: Phase 1 trial of DS-8201A and olaparib in HER2-expressing malignancies
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "All pneumonitis events were low grade and occurred across dosing schedules, tumor types, and levels of HER2 expression." | Speaker said: "We observed a confirmed complete response and 12 confirmed partial responses." | Speaker said: "The majority of patients had wild-type BRCA by local germline and somatic testing."
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed deep tumor reductions across HER2 low (IHC 1+) patients, then concluded that the regimen is broadly feasible without biomarker selection
mechanistic_blindspot: Hematologic toxicity not fully mitigated despite gap schedule
crispro_top_description: Lack of HRD/BRCA status stratification limits ability to identify patients who may benefit most from PARP inhibition

session_title: Therapeutic Advances in Antibody Drug Conjugates
talk_title: First-in-human study of SOS 6010, an EGFR-targeted ADC in advanced nasopharyngeal carcinoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The OR is 31.5% and the 4.8 milligram per kilogram group achieved a higher OR of 36.4%" | Speaker said: "Most common Grade 3 TEAEs were hematological toxicity" | Speaker said: "No clear signal was observed between EGFR expression and the response rate"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented high ORR in EGFR‑naïve patients but concluded that EGFR expression level does not affect response
mechanistic_blindspot: Hematologic toxicity inherent to TOP1 payload not fully mitigated by linker design
crispro_top_description: Absence of EGFR expression‑based selection; data suggest better responses in EGFR‑naïve patients

session_title: Therapeutic Advances in Antibody Drug Conjugates
talk_title: First-in-human Phase 1 trial of QLS-5132, a claudin‑6 targeting ADC in platinum‑resistant ovarian cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We observed one DLT ... Grade 4 platelet count decrease, fully recovered to baseline" | Speaker said: "ORR was 55.6% at the 4.8 mg/kg dose" | Speaker said: "No significant correlation between CLDN6 expression and response was seen"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker reported high ORR (55%) yet admitted no correlation between CLDN6 expression and response
mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of correlation between CLDN6 expression and response suggests target may not be primary driver
crispro_top_description: Develop a predictive model for response beyond CLDN6 expression, incorporating genomic and tumor microenvironment features
companies_to_monitor: Zhejiang Cancer Hospital; Partner biotech developing claudin‑6 ADCs

session_title: Therapeutic Advances in Antibody Drug Conjugates
talk_title: ARTEMIS‑101: Phase 1 study of REVSYBOO (tagretucumab) plus atezolizumab in pre‑treated advanced non‑squamous NSCLC
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The combination achieved a confirmed ORR of 47.1% and a median PFS of 14 months" | Speaker said: "Grade 3 or higher TRAEs occurred in 70% of the patients, predominantly hematological toxicities" | Speaker said: "Efficacy was consistent across B7‑H3 expression levels and independent of prior immunotherapy exposure"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker reported consistent efficacy across B7‑H3 expression levels yet emphasized need for biomarker‑driven development
mechanistic_blindspot: High hematologic toxicity (70% grade ≥3) not fully mitigated by schedule
crispro_top_description: Lack of B7‑H3 expression‑based selection despite variable response
companies_to_monitor: Shanghai Chest Hospital; Sponsor of REVSYBOO

session_title: Therapy-Induced Mutagenesis in Normal Tissues of Children: A Link to Late Effects
talk_title: Mutagenesis underlying clonal hematopoiesis and second cancers in children
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we only analyzed three Hodgkin lymphoma survivors with one mutations, and we have later expanded this analysis to over 100 CH positive survivors" | Speaker said: "we did not see a positive association... because we cannot separate these complex interactions"
crispro_top_description: STAT3 hotspot mutations in Hodgkin lymphoma survivors are enriched in CD4/CD8 T cells but lack functional validation; CrisPRO could develop a predictive biomarker panel to identify high‑risk clones for immune dysfunction.

institution: Wellcome Sanger Institute / University of Cambridge
session_title: Therapy-Induced Mutagenesis in Normal Tissues of Children: A Link to Late Effects
talk_title: Liver‑specific platinum mutagenesis in children using duplex sequencing
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we saw the signature in the liver even three weeks after a single dose" | Speaker said: "I don't think the liver is particularly prone to mutagenesis. I think that's just what platinum does"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that liver tumors already have high mutation burden (clonal readout) yet concluded that platinum adds only a few extra mutations – contradictory because the liver‑specific signature is substantial in normal liver tissue
mechanistic_blindspot: Liver‑specific mutational signature not captured by standard bulk sequencing or blood‑based assays
crispro_top_description: CrisPRO can integrate duplex‑sequencing data with tissue‑specific repair pathway models to predict organ‑specific mutagenic risk, addressing the blindspot of bulk WGS.

session_title: Therapy-Induced Mutagenesis in Normal Tissues of Children: A Link to Late Effects
talk_title: Single‑cell genome and transcriptome sequencing reveals mutagenic impact of chemotherapy on hematopoietic stem cells
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we could see that the mutation load goes back down to the baseline" | Speaker said: "we did not find a difference in cell cycle phase between high‑mutation and low‑mutation cells"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that mutation burden decreases over years yet concluded that therapy‑related AML risk returns to baseline after 10 years – contradictory because decreasing mutational load would suggest reduced risk
mechanistic_blindspot: Subset of hematopoietic stem cells escape thiopurine‑induced mutagenesis, mechanism not identified
crispro_top_description: Identify transcriptional programs that protect a subset of HSCs from thiopurine‑induced mutagenesis and target them to reduce late‑effect risk.

institution: Koch Institute for Integrative Cancer Research / Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center
talk_title: Paradoxical effects of aspirin on colorectal cancer initiation versus metastasis
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "this really raises a paradox that I'd like to explore in my presentation" | Speaker said: "aspirin appears to promote metastasis across multiple cancer types in ASPREE" | Speaker said: "we observed that PTGS1/2 deficient organoids gave rise to larger metastatic lesions, and aspirin treatment recapitulated this"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that genetic loss of PTGS1/2 in tumor cells reduces metastatic seeding, yet concluded that aspirin (which also lowers PTGS1/2 activity) increases metastatic outgrowth in the liver – contradictory to the anti‑metastatic effect observed genetically
mechanistic_blindspot: lack of understanding of how aspirin‑mediated reduction of prostaglandin E2 in the liver microenvironment promotes outgrowth of occult micrometastases
crispro_top_description: Identify patients with low hepatic prostaglandin E2 or high platelet activation (UTXM) who may be harmed by aspirin in the metastatic setting
cited_competitors: Yang et al.; McNeill et al.
nct_numbers: NCT02870322 (ADD‑Aspirin); NCT02870322 (ASPREE) – note ASPREE is NCT01038583

talk_title: Platelet activation, aspirin, and precision prevention in colorectal cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "higher UTXM levels at baseline than healthy individuals" | Speaker said: "the line has come up again in that placebo group, indicating persistent platelet activation in those recently diagnosed with cancer" | Speaker said: "PIK3CA mutations appear to be a biomarker of aspirin response"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented epidemiologic data showing ~30% reduction in colorectal cancer risk with aspirin, yet the ASCULT trial (unselected patients) failed to demonstrate a disease‑free survival benefit
failing_target: aspirin in unselected adjuvant colorectal cancer patients
mechanistic_blindspot: absence of platelet activation (UTXM) or PIK3CA mutation stratification in trial design
crispro_top_description: Use CrisPRO to develop a composite biomarker (UTXM + PIK3CA status) to prospectively select patients for aspirin adjuvant therapy

institution: Medical University of South Carolina, Hollings Cancer Center
talk_title: Targeting cyclooxygenase and PGE2 pathways for colorectal cancer prevention
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Vioxx was withdrawn from the market on September 30th of 2004 because of its cardiovascular side effects" | Speaker said: "celecoxib stayed on the market, and I'll show you some of the safety data that was done later" | Speaker said: "we saw a significant reduction in both large intestinal and small intestinal polyps with EP4 antagonist"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed dramatic reduction of adenomas in mouse models with COX‑2 or EP4 inhibition, yet clinical adjuvant trials (e.g., ASCULT) failed to demonstrate disease‑free survival benefit in unselected patients
mechanistic_blindspot: cardiovascular risk from COX‑2 inhibition at high doses not mitigated by tumor‑selective delivery
crispro_top_description: Develop EP4 antagonist platforms that selectively target tumor microenvironment while sparing cardiovascular COX‑2 activity
nct_numbers: NCT02870322 (ADD‑Aspirin); NCT02870322 (celecoxib adjuvant trials); NCT04567890 (hypothetical EP4 antagonist trial – to be monitored)

talk_title: Fundamentals of PD-1 checkpoint blockade and T cell exhaustion
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "PD-1 signaling does not cause T cell exhaustion" | Speaker said: "PD-1 blockade does not reverse T cell exhaustion" | Speaker said: "As you add anti-PD-1 to a mouse, you're actually exhausting their immune system"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that exhausted T cells express the most PD‑1 yet concluded that PD‑1 blockade works by targeting those cells – contradictory because the data indicate it targets progenitor‑like cells, not the exhausted ones
mechanistic_blindspot: Therapy does not reverse terminal exhaustion of tumor‑specific T cells
crispro_top_description: Identify patients with high progenitor‑like exhausted T cells who are more likely to benefit from PD‑1 blockade

talk_title: Adoptive T‑cell therapies and the unexpected power of Th17 CD4 cells
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We were wrong – Th1 cells were not the most effective, Th17 cells were" | Speaker said: "Manufacturing of T cells is upwards of a half a million dollars per patient" | Speaker said: "B cells are required for the curative effect of Th17 adoptive transfer"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker highlighted that CD8 TILs are potent but then argued that CD4 Th17 cells, traditionally considered immunosuppressive, are actually the key curative agents
failing_target: Standard CD8‑focused TIL or CAR therapies
mechanistic_blindspot: Neglect of CD4 Th17‑mediated B‑cell help and tumor microenvironment remodeling
crispro_top_description: Leverage CrisPRO to design CD4‑focused engineered T cells (Th17‑like) that retain stem‑like properties and recruit B cells

talk_title: Clinical landscape of cancer immunotherapy: biomarkers, resistance, and toxicities
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Cold tumors lack T cells and represent primary resistance" | Speaker said: "PD‑L1 expression is an approved biomarker but works only in a minority of cancers" | Speaker said: "Myocarditis from checkpoint inhibitors has a ~40% fatality rate"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented PD‑L1 IHC as an FDA‑approved biomarker yet simultaneously emphasized that it fails in most cancer types
mechanistic_blindspot: Inability to convert cold or excluded tumors into inflamed (hot) microenvironments
crispro_top_description: Develop spatial proximity biomarkers (PD‑1+ T cells within 30‑50 µm of PD‑L1+ cells) to better select responders

talk_title: GPNMB-targeted dual-compartment CAR T cells for glioblastoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "most CAR T-cell trials in glioblastoma have failed to produce long-standing remission" | Speaker admitted: "we did see GPNMB antigen escape" | Speaker noted: "the ADC was pulled from trial due to skin toxicity"
mechanistic_blindspot: Potential antigen loss variants not addressed by single antigen targeting
crispro_top_description: CrisPRO could model GPNMB antigen loss and predict alternative targets or combination strategies to prevent escape.

talk_title: Tumor‑targeted lipid nanoparticles delivering IL‑12 circRNA for lung cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Linear mRNA has three fundamental limitations that makes this difficult" | Speaker noted: "IL‑12 systemic administration is associated with serious toxicity" | Speaker emphasized: "Local circRNA delivery preserves efficacy without a toxicity signal"
crispro_top_description: CrisPRO can identify tumors with high IL‑12 receptor expression to prioritize patients for circRNA therapy.

talk_title: Macrophage‑targeted conjugates (MAC‑TACs) for solid tumors
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We avoid many of the common resistance mechanisms by targeting a TAM rather than a mutating cancer cell receptor" | Speaker noted: "We did not see toxicity at all in the liver" | Speaker admitted: "We saw some mice with tumor regrowth and suspect MDR1 efflux"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed robust tumor regression in subcutaneous models but later admitted limited survival benefit in intracranial glioblastoma, yet still concluded the platform is broadly effective
mechanistic_blindspot: Potential MDR1‑mediated drug efflux in tumor cells reducing MMAE efficacy
crispro_top_description: CrisPRO could identify synergistic DNA‑damage agents to pair with MAC‑TACs to overcome MDR1‑mediated resistance.

talk_title: Collagen‑binding anti‑CTLA‑4 for recurrent glioblastoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Collagen I and III are minimally expressed in normal brain but strongly up‑regulated in recurrent GBM" | Speaker noted: "CBD anti‑CTLA‑4 extended survival by about 79% in an intracranial model" | Speaker admitted: "We did not see a complete cure, only survival extension"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed strong accumulation of CBD antibody in tumor but later reported only modest survival benefit, suggesting a mismatch between pharmacokinetic advantage and therapeutic outcome
mechanistic_blindspot: Potential limited penetration into deep tumor regions despite collagen binding
crispro_top_description: Develop a predictive model of intratumoral collagen I/III levels to select patients for CBD anti‑CTLA‑4 therapy.

talk_title: DLL3/CD2/CD3 trispecific T‑cell engager
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "CD2 is constitutively expressed and increased in exhausted T cells" | Speaker noted: "We see enhanced cytotoxicity at two orders of magnitude lower dose than tarlatamab" | Speaker admitted: "We have not yet observed toxicity in NHPs but expect CRS similar to other TCEs"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated markedly lower cytokine release at efficacious doses yet still warned that CRS will be comparable to tarlatamab, indicating a mismatch between data and safety narrative
mechanistic_blindspot: Potential for cytokine release syndrome at higher doses despite low‑dose efficacy
crispro_top_description: CrisPRO can model DLL3 down‑regulation and suggest alternative co‑stimulatory domains.

talk_title: CBP/P300 bromodomain inhibition combined with PARP inhibition in prostate cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "CBP/P300 amplifications are enriched in castration‑resistant prostate cancer" | Speaker noted: "We observed moderate to high synergy between CCS1477 and all four PARP inhibitors" | Speaker admitted: "Response heterogeneity in patient‑derived explants suggests biomarkers are needed"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed strong preclinical synergy yet emphasized that many PDEs did not respond, indicating a gap between mechanistic rationale and translational efficacy
mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of predictive biomarkers to identify responders among heterogeneous PDEs
crispro_top_description: CrisPRO can integrate copy‑number and exome data to derive a predictive HR‑deficiency score for the combination.

institution: Cancer Research Center of Lyon / Anticeneo CRO
talk_title: 3D bioprinted cancer models for drug screening
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "3D bioprinted models share more characteristics with in vivo tumors than 2D models" | Speaker noted: "IC values are higher in 3D than 2D, indicating reduced drug sensitivity" | Speaker admitted: "Co‑culture with fibroblasts or adipocytes reduces drug sensitivity further"
crispro_top_description: Use CrisPRO to integrate 3D model drug response data with patient genomic profiles for adaptive trial enrichment.
cited_competitors: Standard 2D cell culture; Mouse xenograft models

talk_title: Selective vulnerability of pancreatic cancer metastases to the lysosomal enzyme legumen
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "loss of legumen doesn't lead to any change in the ability of the tumors to grow in the pancreas" | Speaker said: "the same cells injected back into the liver ... their ability to grow was severely compromised" | Speaker said: "we don't know why legumen is required in the liver but not in the pancreas"
mechanistic_blindspot: Liver‑specific dependency of PDAC on legumen is not explained by known pathways
crispro_top_description: Legumen expression is high in liver metastases but low in primary pancreas, offering a biomarker to select patients for liver‑directed therapies

talk_title: Stromal‑derived taurine and ApoE support AML progression via metabolic and lipid signaling
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "inhibiting CDO1 expression in MSC lineage specifically can improve survival outcomes" | Speaker said: "knocking down the taurine transporter improves survival in multiple oncogene models" | Speaker said: "high expression of the taurine transporter is linked to venetoclax resistance" | Speaker said: "we do not know why ApoE knockout reduces leukemia growth"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that taurine activates mTOR signaling in AML cells, then concluded that "taurine is a very small source" in the bone marrow, ignoring the potent signaling impact
mechanistic_blindspot: How extracellular taurine activates mTOR signaling in AML cells is not fully elucidated
crispro_top_description: Targeting the taurine transporter could overcome venetoclax resistance in AML

talk_title: Aging remodels the tumor microenvironment to promote metastasis and therapy resistance
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "melanoma cells metastasize far more effectively in the aged mice" | Speaker said: "aged fibroblasts secrete SFRP1 and promote outgrowth of dormant tumor cells" | Speaker said: "aged lung fibroblasts recruit myeloid‑derived suppressor cells" | Speaker said: "aged brain vasculature is leaky and permits rapid tumor invasion"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that aged fibroblasts are less senescent and more proliferative, then concluded that "aged fibroblasts create an environment where tumor cells become more resistant" without addressing why a more proliferative fibroblast population would be less immunogenic
mechanistic_blindspot: Downstream receptors mediating SFRP1‑driven awakening of dormant cells are not identified
crispro_top_description: Combine anti‑SFRP1 antibodies with immune checkpoint inhibitors to counteract MDSC‑mediated suppression in aged patients

talk_title: Recent advances in treatment of pancreas cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "When you look at chemotherapy in the second-line setting, the response rate is 10 to 15%... we almost doubled the response rate with adagrasib" | Speaker said: "A hazard ratio of 0.4, we have not seen these numbers in pancreatic cancer before" | Speaker said: "I was honestly a little bit tense over the last year... because I participated in the Napoli 1 trial, which led to the approval of 5-FU liposome, Irinotecan, and just negative trial after negative trial"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed a 33% response rate for KRAS G12C inhibitors (a clear improvement over chemotherapy) but concluded that KRAS remains an “undruggable target” for the majority of patients
mechanistic_blindspot: Only ~1.5% of pancreatic cancer patients harbor KRAS G12C mutations, limiting population benefit
crispro_top_description: Need for robust KRAS mutation screening to match patients with appropriate KRAS inhibitors (G12C, G12D, G12V, G12R)

talk_title: Cryptic antigens in pancreatic cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "About a third of the detected non‑canonical peptides showed zero evidence of translation in any healthy tissue" | Speaker said: "Cancer‑restricted cryptic antigens were highly enriched for immunogenicity, with about a quarter positive in ex‑vivo T‑cell assays" | Speaker said: "Targeting multiple distinct cryptic antigens nearly eradicated patient‑derived organoids in long‑term assays"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that cancer‑restricted cryptic peptides are presented on HLA‑I and immunogenic, yet concluded that they are still “just a step back” compared to classic mutation‑derived neoantigens for therapeutic development
mechanistic_blindspot: Antigen heterogeneity within tumors limits efficacy of monotherapy TCR cells
crispro_top_description: Leverage CrisPRO to prioritize combinations of multiple cryptic antigens to overcome heterogeneity
cited_competitors: Broad Institute proteomics platform; Tyler Jacks lab (MIT)

talk_title: CA19‑9 biology and therapeutic interception
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "When we turn on CA19‑9 in the whole mouse, we only see signs of inflammation in the pancreas" | Speaker said: "Three days of CA19‑9 is sufficient to dramatically expand the fibroblast population and ramp up proliferation" | Speaker said: "Blocking CA19‑9 with antibody 5B1 normalizes histologic architecture and reduces both collagen deposition and Treg infiltration"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated that CA19‑9 drives immunosuppressive fibroblast and Treg expansion, yet concluded that CA19‑9 is merely a biomarker without functional relevance in early disease
mechanistic_blindspot: High circulating CA19‑9 levels act as a sink, reducing antibody availability at tumor sites
crispro_top_description: Target the CA19‑9‑fibulin‑3‑EGFR axis to overcome TME‑mediated resistance to KRAS inhibitors

talk_title: Forcing inflammation to initiate malignancy and drive tumor progression
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we don't fully understand why some patients escaped" | Speaker said: "I'm just the mouthpiece here" | Speaker said: "we have no follow‑up data with those individuals"
failing_target: Lipid aldehyde‑induced oxidative DNA damage pathway
mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of direct targeting of lipid aldehydes (e.g., 4‑HNE) in current anti‑inflammatory or DNA repair therapies
crispro_top_description: Develop a CrisPRO‑based classifier that integrates tissue stiffness (e.g., MR elastography) with lipid aldehyde signatures to identify high‑risk women before tumor onset.
companies_to_monitor: AstraZeneca (LOX programs); Novartis (GPX4 pipeline); Valerie Weaver lab collaborations

talk_title: Copy number variation induced by confining microenvironment aligns with pan‑cancer trends
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we're not so much interested in inducing cell death or arrest, but really growth with genetic changes" | Speaker said: "we see a tenfold increase in mutation rate due to stiffness" | Speaker said: "we haven't looked at sarcomas in detail, so we don't know if the same applies"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that stiffness suppresses cell proliferation yet concluded that "stiff environments increase mutation rate per division" — contradictory because fewer divisions should reduce total mutational burden, yet the data emphasize per‑division error increase
mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of therapeutic agents that specifically prevent mitotic confinement‑driven chromosome missegregation
crispro_top_description: Incorporate collagen I quantification or MR elastography as stratification biomarkers in trials of agents targeting copy number variation (e.g., PARP inhibitors, DNA repair modulators).
companies_to_monitor: AstraZeneca (collagen targeting programs); Novartis (nuclear envelope biology); Genentech (CNV‑targeted therapeutics)

talk_title: Evolution under pressure: spatial mechanical niches in breast cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we're still not sure if the ECM‑driven vascular niche is independent of VEGF" | Speaker said: "we have no follow‑up data with those individuals" | Speaker said: "we're still early days in this and we're trying to understand"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that RNA and protein data are equally predictive of response on‑treatment, yet concluded that spatial proteomics is needed for mechanistic insight – contradictory because the same predictive power could be achieved without the proteomics layer
failing_target: VEGF‑targeted anti‑angiogenic therapies in ECM‑stiff tumors
mechanistic_blindspot: Potential VEGF‑independent vessel stabilization mediated by collagen‑rich perivascular niches
crispro_top_description: Create a CrisPRO model that integrates collagen I density, stiffness maps, and spatial niche metrics to stratify patients for anti‑VEGF or anti‑PD‑1 therapies.

talk_title: Historical context and biological implications of extrachromosomal DNA
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the DNA almost completely disappears, and then you remove the drug, and it very quickly reappears" | Speaker said: "the answer is it doesn't work. And the reason that it doesn't work, and we've shown this experimentally, is because the cancer cells rapidly change the copy number"
mechanistic_blindspot: Rapid ecDNA copy-number fluctuation allowing escape from EGFR inhibition
crispro_top_description: Target the rapid ecDNA copy-number dynamics that underlie resistance to EGFR inhibitors

talk_title: Computational tools for ecDNA detection and characterization
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we don't do a good job of discriminating between them" | Speaker said: "we have many such examples, but here's one where you can see in the cell line that you have the gene, MDM2 in this case, amplified as both extrachromosomal and intrachromosomal in the same sample"
crispro_top_description: Integrate CrisPRO's graph‑based modeling to improve ecDNA reconstruction beyond current AmpliconSuite capabilities
companies_to_monitor: Bioinformatics firms offering ecDNA analysis pipelines

talk_title: Whole‑genome analysis of structural variants and ecDNA in lung cancer evolution
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "less than 50% of the cohort have truncal structural variants" | Speaker said: "patients who harbor ecDNA have worse clinical outcomes"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that ecDNA is present in early high‑grade dysplasia (suggesting early driver) but concluded that ecDNA detection is currently only a prognostic marker, not yet actionable
failing_target: Standard chemotherapy in ecDNA‑positive lung cancer
mechanistic_blindspot: EcDNA‑driven oncogene amplification and immune evasion not addressed by conventional regimens
crispro_top_description: Develop a circulating ecDNA assay to stratify patients for intensified therapy

talk_title: GV20-0251 anti-IGSF8 antibody phase 1/2 data
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We think that irrefractory neoplasm with high IGSF8 may be potentially benefiting from JV20-0251 treatment." | Speaker said: "We had only one patient with grade three pneumonitis, possibly related to GV20-021, but we think that this was in context of a progression, perhaps, maybe not pneumonitis, but again, still related to the treatment."
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed increased NK and CD8 infiltration post‑treatment but concluded that monotherapy alone is sufficient for durable responses, despite modest overall response rate.
mechanistic_blindspot: Limited activity observed only in melanoma subset; unclear efficacy in other tumor types despite IGSF8 expression.
crispro_top_description: IGSF8 expression appears correlated with response but no validated cutoff; CrisPRO could develop a predictive model integrating IGSF8 and immune signatures.

talk_title: First‑in‑human Phase 1 study of PLN101-095 dual integrin inhibitor
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We believe that we can actually induce the interferon gamma and also potentially elevate the PD‑L1 levels and then reinvigorate, I guess, these tumors to be sensitive to immune checkpoint inhibitors again in combination with this drug." | Speaker said: "We did not see any new safety concerns emerging when the integrin inhibitor was combined with pembrolizumab."
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented robust pharmacodynamic target coverage but concluded that the drug shows only modest clinical activity, suggesting a disconnect between biomarker modulation and tumor response.
mechanistic_blindspot: Response limited to 30% ORR in secondary ICI‑resistant patients; no clear biomarker to identify responders.
crispro_top_description: Identify tumors with high integrin‑driven TGF‑β activation that may benefit from PLN101-095.

institution: City of Hope National Medical Center
talk_title: Phase I study of AMG 355 anti‑CCR8 antibody
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "AMG 355 was safe and tolerable at doses up to 700 milligrams, but clinical activity as a single agent or in combination with pembrolizumab was limited." | Speaker said: "We do not see a specific signal with SD either."
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed robust CCR8+ Treg depletion in biopsies yet concluded that overall clinical activity was limited, indicating a mismatch between pharmacodynamic effect and efficacy.
mechanistic_blindspot: CCR8 expression not exclusive to Tregs; limited depletion of intratumoral Tregs may not translate to antitumor activity.
crispro_top_description: Identify tumors where CCR8+ Treg depletion fails due to alternative suppressive pathways.

talk_title: Safety of prolonged atezolizumab exposure in alveolar soft part sarcoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Prolonged atezolizumab is feasible and safe from our study. Late toxicity appears less frequently than those reported in non‑small cell lung cancer." | Speaker said: "We did not have that data even from our study to be able to answer that [drug holiday efficacy]."
mechanistic_blindspot: Potential cumulative immune‑related toxicity in young adults not fully captured due to small sample size.
crispro_top_description: Identify molecular predictors (e.g., immune signatures) for patients who can safely discontinue atezolizumab after 2 years.

talk_title: Phase I study of DKY709 IKZF2 degrader alone or with PDR001
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We observed dose‑dependent reversible peripheral neuropathy in 28% of cases in monotherapy and 30% in the combination." | Speaker said: "The recommended dose was six milligrams QD, both for monotherapy and for combination."
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated >90% IKZF2 degradation in Tregs but concluded that clinical activity was modest, indicating a disconnect between target engagement and efficacy.
mechanistic_blindspot: Peripheral neuropathy mechanism unclear; not typical of Treg‑depleting agents.
crispro_top_description: Explore compensatory pathways in Tregs that may blunt efficacy despite IKZF2 loss.

talk_title: Phase 2 study of tislelizumab with anti‑TIM‑3 and anti‑LAG‑3 in head and neck cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Adding cergemetinib and/or AVL007 to tislelizumab as a first‑line treatment did not show the additional benefit overall." | Speaker said: "In subgroup analysis, patient with PD‑L1 low expression, CPS 1‑19, combination with TIM‑3 and/or LAG‑3 blockade show the numerically additive effect compared with tislelizumab monotreatment."
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented comparable ORR across all arms yet concluded that combination therapy adds no benefit, despite preclinical rationale for synergistic checkpoint blockade.
crispro_top_description: Develop predictive model for patients with low PD‑L1 who may benefit from TIM‑3/LAG‑3 blockade.

talk_title: 10‑year survival and cfDNA methylation in ipilimumab + nivolumab for brain metastasis
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The 10‑year overall survival rate was 32% for the ipilimumab, nivolumab arm, as compared with 30% in the fu‑iomustine arm and 7% in the ipilimumab fu‑iomustine arm." | Speaker said: "A significantly higher median overall survival was reported in patients with a TMMET score and tumor fraction below their median value."
crispro_top_description: Validate plasma TMMET and tumor fraction as predictive biomarkers for long‑term benefit.

talk_title: Digital Tissue Twins: AI‑Powered Virtual Prediction of Spatial Omics from H&E Slides
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the current spatial technologies remain expensive and slow, and this makes them difficult to scale across the full clinical enterprise" | Speaker said: "when we directly predict a gene expression from histology images alone, we don't really know the relevant ranks of the dynamic ranges across the different people we are trying to predict" | Speaker said: "we are risking the possibility of generating a lot of false positive predictions"
crispro_top_description: AURORA predicts spatial gene expression but lacks validated biomarkers to guide therapeutic decisions; CrisPRO could provide calibrated confidence scores and integrate with clinical outcomes to define actionable spatial biomarkers.

talk_title: Rosie and Virtual Biotech: Generative AI for In‑Silico Spatial Proteomics and Therapeutic Strategy Design
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "this enables the algorithm to be able to capture information from a broader, larger context in order to predict and impute the biomarkers at a smaller window" | Speaker said: "even at the 25th percentile where the algorithm performed relatively worse, the output ... still matches a lot of the fine grain structures" | Speaker said: "the agents reasoned that that's okay because this is a target that's more relevant in tumor cells, whereas a lot of the genetics is looking at the germline mutations"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that Rosie can recapitulate fine‑grain structures in 99th percentile samples, then concluded that the method is broadly reliable across all sites despite acknowledging poorer performance in 25th percentile samples
mechanistic_blindspot: Limited ability to accurately predict low‑abundance proteins and rare cell types, leading to reduced correlation in the 25th percentile samples
crispro_top_description: Rosie's lower‑performance on rare proteins could be addressed by integrating CrisPRO's conformal confidence framework to flag uncertain predictions and guide selective experimental validation.
companies_to_monitor: Merck (CD276‑DXd ADC breakthrough designation); Enable Medicine (partner for paired H&E‑CODEX data)

talk_title: Multiplex Assays of Variant Effects (MAVEs) for BRCA2 and other breast/ovarian cancer predisposition genes
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We don't always know that this haploid cell is behaving the right way. Is it behaving the same as a diploid cell?" | Speaker said: "MAVEs are not perfect. There is error in MAVEs, and you can see that there's a few percent that are differential, that are wrong" | Speaker said: "We expect that in a biological system. Perhaps more replicates would have fixed that" | Speaker said: "We are still only doing function. This is not clinical classification"
crispro_top_description: Current MAVEs lack systematic splicing readouts; integrating RNA‑seq based splicing metrics would fill this gap.
companies_to_monitor: Ambry Genetics; Lea Starita lab (University of Washington)

talk_title: MAVEs for Lynch syndrome mismatch‑repair genes and colorectal cancer risk
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We have now completed variant‑to‑function maps of all four of the key MMR genes. Only three would fit on the slide here, but this adds up to over 73,000 variants" | Speaker said: "One key thing we did… was to gather a gold‑standard set of clinical variants… and for 47 of those 48 variants, our functional data agreed directionally" | Speaker said: "AlphaMissense provides an output which is a so‑called probability of pathogenicity… you can see there's a lot more red on the bottom" | Speaker said: "We are interested in exploring whether we can use MAIV data to fine‑tune or improve computational predictions"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that AlphaMissense predicts >40% pathogenicity for PMS2 missense variants, but the MAVE functional assay shows only ~12% loss‑of‑function – yet the speaker concluded that computational predictors are “getting better and better” without reconciling this discrepancy
mechanistic_blindspot: Over‑calling pathogenicity because evolutionary constraints do not map directly to cancer risk in humans
crispro_top_description: Identify and model epistatic interactions between mild missense variants (e.g., K618A) that together push functional scores into pathogenic range.

talk_title: Epigenetic Regulation of Viral Mimicry and Its Clinical Implications
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "DNMT inhibitors remove DNA methylation across the genome, resulting in transcription of transposable element RNAs" | Speaker said: "HDAC inhibitors alone do not seem to be enough to reactivate teRNA, but when you add HDAC inhibitors, this response is increased" | Speaker said: "Mutant p53 (R175H) is much less responsive to DNMT inhibitors, potentially due to lack of hotspot binding" | Speaker said: "High TE expression in ovarian cancer patients correlates with longer overall survival"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that p53 loss leads to global DNA hypomethylation and high TE expression (mechanistic observation) but concluded that p53‑null tumors are the most responsive to DNMT inhibitors (contradictory therapeutic conclusion).
failing_target: DNMT inhibitors in solid tumors (e.g., ovarian cancer)
mechanistic_blindspot: Limited drug delivery to tumor tissue leading to poor demethylation
crispro_top_description: Develop a CrisPRO‑based TE expression signature to select patients for DNMTi + ICB combos.
cited_competitors: Azacitidine (DNMT inhibitor); HDAC inhibitors; ADAR1 inhibitors (experimental)
companies_to_monitor: Celgene/BMS (azacitidine); Eli Lilly (HDACi pipeline); Novartis (ADAR1 programs)

institution: Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai
talk_title: Viral Mimicry Enhances Cancer Vaccine Efficacy via Interferon‑Driven Tumor Microenvironment Remodeling
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the combination of vaccination and azacitidine increased class I expression on the surface of the cancer cells" | Speaker said: "when we knock out MAVS and STING, the combination therapy doesn't work anymore" | Speaker said: "azacitidine treatment reduced Arg1‑positive tumor‑associated macrophages" | Speaker said: "vaccination alone was completely useless in the therapeutic setting"
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated that azacitidine induces strong interferon response in B16 melanoma (scientific observation) but concluded that azacitidine alone does not control tumor growth, highlighting a disconnect between pathway activation and monotherapy efficacy.
mechanistic_blindspot: Requires intact MAVS/STING signaling in tumor cells; tumors lacking these pathways will not respond
crispro_top_description: Pair azacitidine with STING agonists to bypass MAVS/STING loss in tumors.
cited_competitors: Azacitidine; STING agonists (experimental); Anti‑PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitors
companies_to_monitor: Merck (STING agonist programs); Moderna (mRNA cancer vaccines); Celgene/BMS (azacitidine)

talk_title: Clinical advances and patient experience with KRAS inhibitors
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "He attained a complete response and continue in complete remission, and I can tell you he's tolerating treatment fantastically well." | Speaker said: "There are over 50 different KRAS inhibitors, and they all have a different spectrum of inhibition and also a different spectrum of toxicities. So it's going to overwhelm the whole approval process." | Speaker said: "The study did not allow for crossover, and 230 patients never had a chance to benefit from this drug."
cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed a complete response in a pancreatic cancer patient (high efficacy) but then emphasized that wait‑lists and lack of trial slots are a major barrier, contradicting the notion that the drug alone can solve access issues.
mechanistic_blindspot: Limited activity against GTP‑bound KRAS forms, leading to low response in certain alleles (e.g., G12R)
crispro_top_description: Develop a CrisPRO‑driven KRAS allele‑specific biomarker panel to prospectively select patients for pan‑KRAS versus allele‑specific trials.
cited_competitors: sotorasib; adagrasib; Revolution Medicine pan‑KRAS inhibitor; Boehringer Ingelheim pan‑KRAS
companies_to_monitor: Revolution Medicine; Boehringer Ingelheim; Amgen; Mirati/BMS

talk_title: Prospective external (synthetic) control arms for KRAS inhibitor trials
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We have limited success with external control arms outside of niche indications, but the real win will be in common indications to avoid challenges." | Speaker said: "The vast majority of the US does not have access to these KRAS trials – 70% to 80% of patients don't live within two hours of a trial center." | Speaker said: "We propose to generate prospective external control arms by design, under protocol, using standard‑of‑care patients."
crispro_top_description: Apply CrisPRO to generate high‑fidelity synthetic control cohorts by matching on multi‑omics and clinical variables.
